result:
The morning light hit different that Tuesday, slanting through kitchen blinds I'd meant to fix for three years. Coffee gone cold. The cat knocking a pen off the table with that deliberate malice only cats possess, watching it fall with the patience of something that doesn't pay rent.

I've been thinking about the weight of unwritten letters. Not emails. Letters. The kind you start in your head at 2 AM, addressing someone from fifteen years ago, someone who wouldn't recognize your handwriting now. There's a specific gravity to them, these phantom correspondences. They accumulate. You carry them in your jaw, your shoulders, that spot between your shoulder blades where stress nests like a bird you can't evict.

My grandmother saved every letter my grandfather wrote her during Korea. Found them in a shoebox after she died, tied with ribbon that crumbled when I touched it. His handwriting changed over those two years—you could watch a man become someone else in the slant of his gs, the pressure of his pen. Early ones: exuberant loops, exclamation points like fireworks. Later: smaller, tighter, words pressed into the page as if he could force meaning through sheer indentation. He never talked about the war. Not once in forty years of marriage. But there it was, in the physics of his script, the chemistry of ink on paper that had gone the color of old teeth.

We think we're so permanent now, don't we? Cloud storage and redundant backups and the illusion that anything posted stays posted. But I've had three hard drives fail, two email accounts evaporate when companies folded, a phone stolen with two years of photos I thought I'd transferred but hadn't. The shoebox outlasted all of it. The shoebox and its ribbon, becoming dust, becoming memory held together by the fact of my witnessing.

There's a bakery on Fourth where the owner still writes orders by hand, carbon paper between sheets, that particular smell of chemical duplication. I go there when I need to remember that time has texture. She hums opera I don't recognize, off-key, while kneading dough that will become someone's Wednesday morning, someone's small comfort before a meeting they dread. I've never asked her name. Some presences don't require introduction.

I used to write in cafes with more intention. The performance of it, maybe. Laptop open, Americano going cold, crafting sentences that felt important in the moment and embarrassing by evening. Now I write on the bus, standing, one hand gripping the rail, thumb typing into notes I'll never revisit. The form follows the fracture. We're all broken into smaller and smaller attention spans, and I can't decide if that's tragedy or just evolution, if there's a meaningful difference.

My neighbor practices saxophone at odd hours. Badly. The same four measures of something jazz-adjacent, over and over, the missed note always in the same place, that particular squeak of reed and desperation. I should be annoyed. I am not. There is something holy in his persistence, in the willingness to be bad at something in a world that only broadcasts expertise. I want to tell him this. I never will. We pass in the hallway with the nod of people who share walls, who hear each other's arguments and plumbing and the particular rhythm of our separate insomnias.

The cat has moved on to knocking a bottle cap across the floor. This is his kingdom. I merely pay for it.

What I meant to say, I think, is that I've been noticing the edges of things more lately. The way light pools in a scratched countertop. The particular silence of a house when you've been alone in it long enough that you start talking to yourself, answering, having full conversations about whether to buy more milk. The way grief sits in a body—not dramatically, not cinematically, but as a slight wrongness in how you hold your shoulders, a hesitation before reaching for something you used to share.

My friend died last spring. Not COVID, just the ordinary catastrophe of cells deciding to replicate without permission. We hadn't spoken in two years, one of those silences that starts with busyness and becomes something else, becomes a wall you can't remember building. I found out from a Facebook post his sister made, which I saw while waiting for a prescription, standing in CVS between the greeting cards and the mini shampoo bottles. Happy birthday, she wrote. We miss you. And I stood there with my phone in my hand understanding that I would now carry this forever, this specific failure, this particular way I was not there.

I still have his number in my phone. I can't delete it. I can't call it either. It exists in the same category as those 2 AM letters, the ones composed in the dark and never sent.

The morning has moved on without me. The coffee is definitely cold now. The cat sleeps in the patch of sun that has migrated across the floor, following the earth's rotation with the instinct of something that doesn't know it's on a planet, doesn't need to know, is present in a way I can't access and both envy and pity.

I read somewhere that trees communicate through fungal networks, the mycorrhizal web, sending chemical signals, sharing nutrients, warning of insect attack. The "wood wide web," scientists call it, with the terrible punning instinct of people who spend too much time alone in labs. But I can't stop thinking about it. The forest as something more than collection, as conversation, as care. What would we hear if we could listen? Probably nothing that translates. Probably something that would break our hearts with its difference, its complete indifference to our metaphors.

I'm running out of words now, approaching some limit I set for myself, though the counting is approximate, though I've never been precise about anything in my life. What remains? The afternoon stretching ahead with its ordinary demands. The cat's eventual hunger. The possibility, always the possibility, of starting something I won't finish, of reaching toward someone I haven't spoken to, of writing the letter that doesn't stay in my head but finds paper, finds stamp, finds its way through the vast machinery of delivery to a mailbox that might still be checked with something like hope.

The light has moved off the counter. The blinds still need fixing. Everything needs fixing, always, and we keep going anyway, making our small sounds in the larger silence, our practice-saxophone persistence, our bad notes held with something like faith that the next one might be better, that the measure might eventually complete itself, that someone, somewhere, is listening without judgment, is glad simply for the proof of effort, the evidence that we were here, that we tried, that we continue in our broken beautiful way to try.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
